audience: Students, educators, and families in distance learning communities

hero.title: If remote learning feels too big, start with one small next step.
hero.subtitle: This page is designed first for learners. Choose the support you need now, then let the adults helping you go deeper through their own tab.
hero.quickStartTitle: Start with what is true right now
quickStartSteps: Choose the route that matches the current problem.
Use only the section you need.
Reach out to a person if the barrier is bigger than the task.
hero.quickStartNote: The goal is orientation first, then progress.

journeyCards.title: I am a learner and need help now
journeyCards.description: Go to the support choices, reduce overload, and find the right words or contact route.

journeyCards.title: I want to make the day manageable
journeyCards.description: See what matters today, how remote learning usually works, and what routines help most.

journeyCards.title: I need calm, comfort, or a person
journeyCards.description: Go straight to regulation steps, wellbeing support, and human contact routes.

studentIntents.label: I feel overwhelmed
studentIntents.title: Make the pressure smaller first
studentIntents.reassurance: You do not need to win the whole day right now. The goal is to reduce overload until one next step feels manageable again.
studentIntents.nextStep: Pause the full list, take one slow breath, and choose one action only.
studentIntents.followUp: If the pressure stays high, switch from task-solving to contacting a trusted adult or support lead.
studentIntents.script: I am overwhelmed and need help to find the next small step.
visualItems: Look away from the whole list.
Ask for help if it still feels too big.
studentIntents.contactValue: Add the right pastoral or student-support contact here

studentIntents.label: I can't get started
studentIntents.title: Begin with the easiest visible action
studentIntents.reassurance: Starting often feels harder than continuing. Progress usually begins after a tiny first action, not before it.
studentIntents.nextStep: Open the task, write the first action in your own words, and do only that one part.
studentIntents.followUp: If the first action is still unclear, message the right adult and ask for the next step in a smaller chunk.
studentIntents.script: I can see the work, but I need one clearer first step to begin.
studentIntents.contactValue: Add your preferred teacher contact route

studentIntents.label: I'm confused
studentIntents.title: Get clarity before you keep going
studentIntents.reassurance: Confusion usually means the route into the task needs to be clearer, smaller, or explained another way.
studentIntents.nextStep: Read the instruction out loud, find one word you recognise, and ask what the first step is.
studentIntents.followUp: If reading is part of the problem, ask for read-aloud, verbal explanation, or visual chunking.
studentIntents.script: I am not sure what the first step is. Could you make it smaller or clearer?
studentIntents.contactValue: Add your organisation's support route here

studentIntents.label: I need calm
studentIntents.title: Regulate before trying to perform
studentIntents.reassurance: When the body is stressed, the next step is regulation first. Calm support usually works better than more pressure.
studentIntents.nextStep: Slow the breath, relax the shoulders, and choose one short reset before returning to the screen.
studentIntents.followUp: If you still feel too activated or flat, use a wellbeing or pastoral contact instead of staying stuck alone.
studentIntents.script: I need a short reset before I can rejoin properly.
visualItems: Notice what your body needs first.
studentIntents.contactValue: Add the right wellbeing or pastoral escalation route

studentIntents.label: I need a person
studentIntents.title: Go straight to a human support route
studentIntents.reassurance: Some barriers need a person more than a checklist. Use a human support route early if the day is becoming too hard.
studentIntents.nextStep: Send one short message describing the barrier and asking for the next step.
studentIntents.followUp: If one contact route does not work quickly enough, try another trusted adult or support lead next.
studentIntents.script: I need an adult to help me because this is affecting my day.
visualItems: Say what is wrong in one sentence.
Follow up with another support contact if needed.
studentIntents.contactValue: Add the right wellbeing or pastoral escalation route

headings.journeyTitle: Start with the need, not the whole site
headings.journeyCaption: Different users arrive with different levels of stress and responsibility. Route them quickly to the right support.
headings.intentPanelCaption: Choose the closest match. Each path gives one next step, a visual guide, and a person to contact if the barrier is bigger than the task.
headings.supportTitle: What do you need right now?
headings.supportCaption: Choose the closest match. Read one panel only, then act on the next step.
headings.supportTeamCaption: Use visible support routes before a learner disappears into silence or overload.
headings.dayTitle: How to structure the remote day
headings.phraseCaption: Simple scripts lower the barrier to asking for help.
headings.routineCaption: The aim is steady participation, not performing calm while struggling.
headings.educatorTitle: For teachers, tutors, parents, and carers
headings.educatorCaption: These sections support adults who are helping learners regulate, rejoin, or access work more effectively.
headings.accessibilityCaption: Support improves when language is lighter, choices are clearer, and access routes are flexible.
headings.wellbeingCaption: Keep regulation, contact, and emotional support easy to find when the day starts to feel too heavy.
headings.supportPageTitle: When the day is becoming too much
headings.supportPageIntro: Keep regulation, contact, and safety visible alongside academic guidance.
headings.adultSummaryTitle: Use this section to support the student journey, not replace it
headings.adultSummaryText: Keep the learner-facing routes calm and visible first. Then use this adult guidance to lower barriers, support re-entry, and adapt access without turning the whole page into adult-mode navigation.

helperCards.title: I feel overwhelmed
steps: Pause and breathe more slowly.
Look away from the full to-do list.
Choose one small action only.
Ask for help if the task still feels too big.
helperCards.note: Start by making the demand smaller.

helperCards.title: I cannot focus
steps: Close extra tabs.
Put your phone away.
Set a short timer.
Work only on the first instruction.

helperCards.title: I cannot get started
steps: Open the lesson or task.
Write down the first action in your own words.
Do that one step only.
Contact someone if the first step is still unclear.
helperCards.note: Starting is often harder than continuing.

helperCards.title: I do not understand the task
steps: Read the instruction out loud.
Find one word you recognise.
Ask what the first step is.
Request a smaller chunk if needed.
helperCards.note: Clarity often comes after asking one precise question.

helperCards.title: Reading on screen is hard
steps: Use read-aloud or text-to-speech tools.
Read one line at a time.
Ask for instructions in smaller parts.
Use audio, verbal explanation, or visual structure where possible.
helperCards.note: Different access routes are still valid learning routes.

helperCards.title: I need an adult to help me
steps: Message the right teacher, tutor, or learning support contact.
Describe the problem in one sentence.
Say whether the barrier is stress, access, understanding, or time.
helperCards.note: Support works best when it is explicit and early.

dayExpectations: Check the main learning platform daily.
Use a visible routine for lessons, breaks, and tasks.
Communicate early when something is becoming a barrier.
Rejoining matters more than catching up perfectly.
lessonRules: Online learning may include live teaching, posted tasks, messages, or email.
Keep one clear place for timetables, deadlines, and notes.
Use contact routes early if a learner cannot access the task or session.
onlineExpectations: Show up ready to learn where possible.
Use support routes before stress becomes withdrawal.
Aim for steady participation, not perfection.

supportPhrases.text: I am not sure what the first step is.
Could you make that smaller or clearer?
I am overwhelmed and need help to start.
I cannot access this in the way it is written.

supportBullets: Reduce cognitive load before repeating instructions.
Keep contact routes obvious and human.
Prefer one clear next step over long lists.
wellbeingHabits: Keep a predictable rhythm for sleep, meals, and breaks.
Step away from the screen before overload builds.
Treat rest and regulation as part of learning.
wellbeingResetSteps: Notice what the body needs first.
Choose one action or one person to contact.

wellbeingContacts.label: Pastoral support
wellbeingContacts.value: Add the right pastoral or student-support contact here

wellbeingContacts.label: Learning support
wellbeingContacts.value: Add the local support contact here

wellbeingResources.title: Breathing exercise
wellbeingResources.description: Replace with a trusted local regulation or breathing resource.

wellbeingResources.title: Movement break
wellbeingResources.description: Replace with a short stretching or reset resource.

supportContacts.label: Learning support
supportContacts.value: Add your organisation's support route here

supportContacts.label: Teacher or tutor
supportContacts.value: Add your preferred teacher contact route

supportContacts.label: Pastoral lead
supportContacts.value: Add the right wellbeing or pastoral escalation route

educatorCards.title: When a learner is dysregulated
educatorCards.summary: Reduce demand first. A regulated learner can re-engage more effectively than a pressured one.
steps: Acknowledge the stress without escalating it.
Offer one clear next step.
Reduce reading or task volume temporarily.
Reconnect the learner with the right support route if the barrier is emotional.

educatorCards.title: When a learner is disengaging
educatorCards.summary: Treat silence, lateness, or repeated absence as information, not just non-compliance.
steps: Check for access, anxiety, executive function, and understanding barriers.
Make the re-entry step simple and achievable.
Use support routes early rather than after prolonged absence.
Avoid framing rejoining as failure.

educatorCards.title: When additional needs affect access
educatorCards.summary: Adapt the route into the work, not just the encouragement around it.
steps: Shorten instructions and separate steps clearly.
Offer read-aloud, visual chunking, or verbal restatement.
Keep expectations consistent but presentation flexible.
Distinguish between motivation issues and access issues.

accessibilityFeatures.title: Lower the reading burden
accessibilityFeatures.description: Use shorter blocks, plain language, and one action at a time.

accessibilityFeatures.title: Offer more than one access route
accessibilityFeatures.description: Support text, audio, visual structure, and spoken clarification wherever possible.

accessibilityFeatures.title: Keep navigation predictable
accessibilityFeatures.description: Make it easy to find urgent help, daily routine guidance, and adult support without scanning the full site.

accessibilityFeatures.title: Make help scriptable
accessibilityFeatures.description: Learners often need exact words they can reuse when they are stressed or unsure.

footer.title: Support works best when the next step feels possible.
footer.text: Remote learning becomes more manageable when help is calm, clear, and easy to reach for learners and the adults around them.
footer.note: If a learner is stuck, ask a trusted adult, teacher, or support lead for the next small step.

adultFooter.title: Make re-entry feel possible, not punitive.
adultFooter.text: Adults can lower stress by simplifying the route back in, checking access needs early, and giving the learner one clear next action instead of a long list.
adultFooter.note: If the barrier continues, involve the right support lead or pastoral contact before the learner becomes more disconnected.
